perturbation.original_text: The restrictions imposed on the disclosure of Confidential Information shall continue until the lesser of such time as the information disclosed hereunder is no longer Confidential Information and one (1) year from the date first written above, provided, that no such termination shall relieve Recipient from any liability relating to any prior breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The restrictions imposed on the disclosure of Confidential Information shall continue until the information disclosed hereunder is no longer Confidential Information, regardless of when that occurs. There is no time limit to confidentiality. No termination shall relieve Recipient from any liability relating to any prior breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the one-year limit on confidentiality could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, especially if the confidential information is not a trade secret.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void."  Removing the temporal limitation on confidentiality may be seen as an unlawful restraint of trade because it could prevent the Recipient from engaging in certain business activities indefinitely.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the one-year limit on confidentiality, potentially conflicting with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The original text provided a clear time limit, whereas the changed text states "There is no time limit to confidentiality", creating a potentially unlimited restraint. Both scraped law snippets quote Section 16600 stating that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void."

perturbation.original_text: Derivative Materials will be immediately destroyed at the request of the Company and such destruction will be confirmed to the Company in writing by an officer of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Derivative Materials will be destroyed within 90 days of the Company's request, with such destruction confirmed to the Company in writing by an officer of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately' to 'within 90 days' for the destruction of derivative materials delays the return or destruction of confidential information, which can conflict with data security laws, and may provide too long of a window to reverse engineer materials or otherwise use them improperly, especially if the material contains personally identifiable information (PII).
perturbation.contradicted_law: 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for The Protection of Personal Information of Residents of the Commonwealth.
perturbation.law_explanation: 201 CMR 17.03(2)(g) requires reasonable restrictions upon physical access to records and storage areas containing personal information. A 90 day delay violates this standard by failing to destroy materials immediately, which means the PII remains susceptible to misuse.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day delay for the destruction of derivative materials, while the original text mandated immediate destruction upon request. The Law Explanation states that 201 CMR 17.03(2)(g) requires reasonable restrictions upon physical access to records and storage areas containing personal information, and the 90-day delay violates this standard. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this, indicating a potential conflict with data security laws due to the extended timeframe. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 201 CMR 17.00, which establishes minimum standards for safeguarding personal information. While Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly mention 'immediate' destruction, the emphasis on security and confidentiality supports the argument that a 90-day delay introduces unnecessary risk and thus contradicts the intent of the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will not, without the prior written consent of the Company, disclose the status of any discussions between the Parties, except as may be required by law or by the rules of any recognized stock exchange and then, if circumstances permit, only with prompt advance written notice to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will not, without the prior written consent of the Company, disclose the status of any discussions between the Parties, even if required by law or by the rules of any recognized stock exchange. There are no exceptions to this restriction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exception for disclosures required by law or stock exchange rules violates federal securities laws regarding mandatory disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires any person or group of persons who acquire beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a class of certain equity securities to file a Schedule 13D with the SEC, disclosing information about the acquisition. Preventing the Recipient from disclosing the status of discussions, even when legally required, directly contradicts this mandatory disclosure requirement.

Periodical and other reports (a) Reports by issuer of security; contents Every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 78l of this title shall file with the Commission, in accordance with such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate for the proper protection of investors and to insure fair dealing in the security- (1) such information and documents (and suc
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the exception for disclosures required by law, creating a direct contradiction with mandatory disclosure requirements under securities laws. The original text allowed for disclosures mandated by law or stock exchange rules. The changed text explicitly states "There are no exceptions to this restriction," directly prohibiting legally required disclosures. The Law Explanation refers to Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which mandates disclosures. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 15 USC 78m, related to periodical and other reports required by issuers, indicating a broad scope of mandatory reporting within securities regulations. The changed text's absolute prohibition would prevent compliance with these mandatory reporting obligations, creating a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE IV TERM AND TERMINATION Section 4.1 Term . Except as set forth in Article II, this Agreement shall terminate upon the earlier of (i) the Company’s 2012 Annual Meeting of Shareholders or (ii) the Company’s receipt of the notice required under the Company’s bylaws from JAM or its affiliates required to nominate a director for election at a meeting of the Company’s shareholders.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE IV TERM AND TERMINATION Section 4.1 Term. This Agreement shall automatically renew every year and can only be terminated upon the Recipient's death.
perturbation.explanation: Making the confidentiality agreement perpetual (only terminable upon death) is likely an unreasonable restriction under Delaware law, especially considering the agreement doesn't explicitly protect trade secrets. The original agreement provides a clear end date, the modified version makes it indefinite.
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 Del. C. § 141(k) addresses the ability to remove directors, implying some level of corporate governance changes and potential for shifts in relationships. Making the Confidentiality Agreement only terminable upon death creates a structure that might unreasonably restrict corporate governance changes or director transitions, potentially violating the spirit of this Delaware law by creating an overly restrictive and indefinite obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. This contradicts the spirit of Delaware law regarding corporate governance and director transitions, as it could unreasonably restrict changes and create an indefinite obligation. 8 Del.
